articles.title: China Manufacturing Contracts: Why Your Draft Does Not Work
articles.content:
客户经常向我们发送美国制造协议，询问是否能节省中国制造合同的时间或成本。答案是不能。有时问题更小：能否花一两个小时审阅一下？我们拒绝，因为我们不会为明知会被浪费的时间向客户收费。我们可能会花十到十五分钟看看他们已有的内容，以便直接讨论。然后我们告诉他们我们一贯发现的事实：使用它不会减少我们的时间，反而会增加。美国起草的草案基于在美国本土才能适用的执行假设，在中国大陆则无法适用。美国起草律师可能做了扎实的本土工作，但中国制造需要为在中国执行而起草的合同。我们现在收到关于人工智能生成草案的同样请求。它们失败的原因相同，而且还有许多其自身特有的原因。我们最近审阅了这样一份草案。它有熟悉的标题：知识产权、工装模具、保修、合规、争议解决、付款、交货和终止。但标题下的条款给予买方的保护远低于其所需。美国律师经常使用熟悉的工具：英语仲裁、纽约或香港法律、广泛赔偿、保险要求和美式救济。这些条款在对中国工厂执行时很少能帮助买方。中国制造合同应根据其在工厂违约时能够支持的救济措施来评判。买方能否冻结当地资产？正确的中国实体是否受到约束？买方能否在其设计被用于他人生产之前取回其模具、工装或专有材料？如果合同无法支持这些救济措施，它提供的杠杆作用就很小。

客户经常问我们的中国制造合同是否可以在中国法院执行。是的，可以，但如果你不得不起诉你的制造商，事情已经出问题了。我们起草这些合同是为了在中国可执行，因为预期会在中国法院败诉的制造商违约可能性大大降低。最好的中国制造合同是能够阻止诉讼的那个。

对比很明确：

争议解决：美国草案通常默认香港或美国仲裁（使用英语），或在美国本地法院诉讼。中国制造合同通常需要在执行地有PRC法院或中国境内仲裁。

准据法：美国草案通常使用纽约或香港法律。对于中国大陆制造，PRC法律通常更合理。

语言：美国草案通常规定英语优先。对于中国执行，中文版本通常应该优先。

损害赔偿：美国草案通常依赖日后证明实际损害。中国制造合同应使用与特定违约挂钩的特定合同损害赔偿。

执行：美国草案通常假设买方会先胜诉然后追偿。中国制造合同应保留提前冻结资产和创造杠杆的能力。

知识产权保护：美国草案通常依赖通用保密和所有权语言。中国制造合同需要NNN、不使用、反规避和禁止未经授权的中国知识产权申请。

我们审阅的草案要求适用田纳西州法律，英语作为控制语言，管辖权在纳什维尔州法院。这些选择适合美国本土交易。当供应商、工厂和资产都在中国大陆时，这些选择几乎没有作用。中国制造协议通常应该为中国执行而构建：PRC法律、中国论坛和控制中文版本。没有这种结构，买方很可能拥有一份在执行时几乎没有帮助的合同。

关于更多细节，请参阅《如何起草可执行的中国合同》。争议条款控制着买方在模具和资金消失之前能够多快采取行动。国内模板的样板条款是不够的。香港和新加坡仲裁是区域商业协议中的常见默认选择。对于中国大陆制造，它们增加了延迟和成本。如果制造商及其资产在中国大陆，中国法院适用PRC法律对中文合同可以直接给予买方诉前财产保全、银行账户冻结、禁令以及模具和设备扣押的访问权。时间很重要，因为争议很快就会变成追偿问题。中国大陆以外的仲裁通常会增加第二步执行。买方必须首先在香港或新加坡获得仲裁裁决，然后请求中国大陆法院承认和执行该裁决以对抗工厂的当地资产。当供应商、银行账户、工厂和工装都在中国大陆时，这额外的一步可能使买方损失时间和杠杆。

如果诉讼发生在中国，法院将使用中文工作。仅英文合同可能需要在争议开始后翻译，届时技术术语、产品规格和法律措辞已经存在争议。到那时，每个歧义都会花费更多。如果英文和中文版本内容不同，合同应规定哪个版本优先。翻译本身还不够。中文条款必须对制造商和法官都有意义。英文版本仍需反映中国执行策略，中文版本需要与之匹配。

未能以官方中文法定名称识别供应商会造成严重的执行风险。发票、网站上的英文名称通常不是注册的法定名称，使用错误的名称可能使合同难以或无法执行。合同需要约束持有营业执照、资产、银行账户和工厂运营的确切中国实体。我们验证准确的中文名称以确保合同约束我们可能需要起诉或执行的实体。可执行的当事方是中国法律实体，而非其文件上的英文名称。公司印章是关键的执行工具。仅由个人签署的合同可能引发签署人缺乏权限的争议。由正确的人签署并盖有正确印章的合同更难被供应商否认。该协议还应控制谁执行工作以及资金流向何处。买方有时在太晚的时候才发现他们认为使用的工厂不是生产产品的工厂。其他人发现付款流向了香港账户、个人账户、关联公司账户，或合同方后来声称不当的另一个账户。这些问题直接关系到可执行性、杠杆和欺诈预防。美国式合同通常不解决这些中国特有的风险。

美国保密协议旨在阻止披露。在中国制造中，更大的风险通常是非授权使用。工厂可能利用买方的图纸、工装、包装、样品或客户信息，通过关联公司、分包商或直销渠道生产竞争产品。中国保密协议应涵盖非使用、非披露和非规避，而不将这些概念变成样板条款。保密条款必须限制工厂使用买方的设计、模具、样品、CAD文件、技术信息、客户信息和相关材料。它们还必须涵盖工厂的关联公司、分包商、零部件供应商、相关公司和副业。命名它们通常是明智的。美国式保密条款可能保护“保密信息”，但很少控制中国工厂对买方设计的处理方式。参见《中国保密协议：这么多年后仍然毫无价值》。

该协议还应防止供应商、其关联公司、所有者、员工和分包商基于买方的品牌、产品或设计在中国申请商标、外观设计专利、版权、域名或类似申请。中国的先申请商标制度使这一点尤为重要。如果买方未在中国注册其商标，工厂或关联公司可能试图首先注册并利用该注册作为杠杆。合同应禁止这种行为，但更好的答案通常是在工厂有机会之前就在中国注册商标。参见《中国商标注册时机：恰到好处原则》。

我们审阅的草案禁止销售“相同或类似产品”，但从未说明“类似”的含义。被指控复制或修改产品的工厂可以辩称新版本不在限制范围内。定义可能需要涵盖使用相同工装、基于相同设计文件、使用受保护组件、复制买方包装或商业外观或包含相同面向客户功能的产品。中国法院不会为买方填补这一空白，因此限制必须在合同中。

草案要求中国制造商支付保证金以确保工装返回，只有在工装完好归还后才能退还。这听起来可能不错，但除非买方拥有重大杠杆，否则几乎所有中国工厂都会拒绝。工装和模具保护最好通过物理和合同控制来实现：明确的所有权语言、适当的标记或序列号、位置限制、返回期限、使用限制，以及拒绝归还或未经授权使用的明确定义合同损害赔偿。目标是工厂会接受的条款和中国法院会执行的条款。参见《国际制造中模具和工装完整指南》。

风险在生产开始之前就开始了。工厂可能在第一份采购订单发出之前就收到图纸、CAD文件、样品、包装设计、徽标、模具、定价数据和技术规格。到那时，买方可能已经转移了其最敏感的信息。包装值得单独保护。它通常包含商标、艺术品、警告、条形码、合规标记和面向客户的设计。滥用包装的工厂可能制造假冒产品、灰色市场商品、保证混淆、海关问题和声誉损害。通用知识产权语言无法充分控制这些风险。

PRC法院可能会调整他们认为相对于实际损失过高的合同损害赔偿。目标是为特定违约（如延迟交货、工装保留、未经授权分包或NNN违规）设定足够实质性以创造杠杆且足够可信以供中国法院执行的损害赔偿。正确设定数字需要经验和背景。我们看到的大多数合同草案要么省略合同损害赔偿，要么使用数字偏差太大以至于条款可能弊大于利。对于延迟交货，合同可能使用订单价值的每日百分比。对于未能归还工装，可能使用固定金额或更换成本的百分比。对于NNN或知识产权违规，可能设定每次违规的最低金额。正确的结构取决于产品、订单价值和可能的损害。当中国制造商违约时，买方需要的不仅仅是一般起诉权。它需要能够在损害变得不可挽回之前创造杠杆的救济措施。

我们审阅的草案使用了从验收起24个月的保修期，并指出“正确期限取决于产品类别和销售周期”。这正是问题所在：中国制造没有标准保修期。正确的保修取决于产品、销售渠道、缺陷模式、使用寿命、运输时间表和缺陷出现时间。从工厂验收开始计算的保修期可能对在运输、仓储或季节性零售周期中花费数月的产品来说太短了。对于某些产品，保修应从交货、零售、安装或首次使用开始计算。

质量控制条款也存在同样的问题。大多数工厂争议都围绕具体事实：缺陷率、美容缺陷与实质性缺陷、潜在故障或勉强未通过检验的装运。可行的质量条款使用可衡量的标准：AQL水平、缺陷分类、批准样品、测试协议、重新检验权、装运暂停，以及工厂支付失败检验和重新检验费用的明确规则。通过发货前检验不应意味着最终验收。有些缺陷只在组装、安装、销售或使用后才出现。如果合同将检验视为验收，买方可能在知道产品是否正常之前就失去杠杆。中国工厂有时会更换材料、使用更便宜的组件、转移生产或外包工作。他们可能在削减成本、处理超负荷，或掩盖他们从未有过的能力。



合同需要明确的生产基准：已批准的物料清单、参考样品、零部件规格、已批准的供应商、生产地点以及书面变更控制程序。如果没有这个基准，工厂可以辩称更便宜的替代品、不同供应商或变更的生产方法已经足够接近。

大多数中国制造合同也应禁止分包、生产转移、供应商变更以及未经买方书面批准的材料或零部件替代。这些限制应有明确的合同损害赔偿作为后盾。否则，当仿冒产品出现或生产转移到未经授权的工厂时，工厂可以归咎于“另一家公司”，让买家追错对象。

对于消费品，召回条款至关重要。通用保修条款无法涵盖召回合作、调查、纠正措施、监管沟通、成本分配、赔偿或对缺陷或不合规商品的责任。当产品在市场上出现问题时，买方通常首先面对客户、零售商、监管机构或原告。合同需要将适当的责任推回给制造商，因为召回风险可能远远超过缺陷商品的成本。

替换零件同样重要。合同应明确规定工厂是否必须提供备件，提供多长时间，价格多少，数量多少，以及时间表。还应涵盖保修索赔、维修、召回和停产产品所需的零件。我们看到的大多数草案完全遗漏了这一点。

许多争议源于操作模糊性：采购订单接受、交货日期、价格变动、检验时间、装运批准、发票条款和付款条件。中国制造协议应直接解决这些问题，而不是留给电子邮件或发票处理。我们通常要求制造商在规定期限内书面接受或拒绝每个采购订单，沉默视为拒绝。这可以防止制造商拖延订单，然后归咎于买方延误。

常见的30%定金和70%装运前付款结构使买方在装运后几乎没有筹码。一旦买方在货物离开中国前支付了100%的款项，对于保修索赔、缺陷、缺失文件或纠正措施就几乎没有压力了。在可能的情况下，买方应保留部分货款，直到目的地检验、交货或规定的到货后期限。即使是适度的扣留也可以改变工厂的激励机制。付款应只支付给签约的中国公司，且只支付到批准的银行账户。支付到香港账户、个人账户、经纪账户或关联公司账户可能造成严重的执行和欺诈问题。

延迟交货需要同样的纪律。一条款项说交货日期是固定的，延迟交货可能造成损害“如果适用”，这只是陈述了一个期望而没有提供补救。合同应明确规定每日合同损害赔偿、取消权、退款、加急运输义务、扣款、替代购买权和终止权。

我们审查的草案包含详细的美国法规合规部分，涵盖CPSC、ASTM、第三方测试报告、合格证书、美国海关文件，以及对缺失认证扣留最终付款的权利。但它仍然需要与实际产品、标准、测试义务、文件、测试失败后果和进口要求相联系。合规条款应分配测试成本、报告截止日期、适用标准、测试失败后果、进口风险、不准确证书和测试后的产品变更。

合同还应处理进口文件、关税分类支持、原产地问题、强迫劳动合规、制裁限制、准确的商业发票、装箱单以及与海关查询的合作。不良文件可能导致货物延误、触发扣押、产生罚款，或让买方为工厂造成的问题承担责任。当产品产生海关或强迫劳动风险时，笼统承诺“遵守所有法律”是不够的。合同可能需要可追溯性记录、零部件原产地文件、供应商认证和原材料文件。它还应将这些风险分配给工厂，并在工厂造成进口或海关问题时给予买方补救。

草案合同的保险部分要求商业一般责任险、产品责任险、空白美元限额、附加被保险人条款以及装运前的保险证明。要求中国的工厂将买方添加为附加被保险人很少提供实际保护。工厂可能会将保险成本计入产品价格，让买方间接支付一份未经审查的、可能难以执行的中文保单。我们的中国律师经常看到这种情况。一家公司告诉我们其中国工厂有保险，但当我们询问是否有人读过中文保单时，答案通常是否定的，或者是的但没有人真正知道其内容。如果产品责任保险很重要，买方通常最好通过自己的经纪人购买保险，在一份可以理解和控制的保单下获得保障。对工厂的保护来自合同损害赔偿、付款杠杆以及追索工厂在中国资产的能力。

赔偿条款也遗漏了重要风险。它涵盖了产品缺陷、违约和过失或故意不当行为，但几乎没有其他内容。例如，工厂可能违反与另一个买家的独家安排，并将新买家拖入该纠纷。工厂应为此向买家赔偿。赔偿条款还应涵盖知识产权索赔、监管和海关问题、强迫劳动问题、召回、分包商不当行为、未经授权的材料、不准确的认证、滥用买方财产和辩护程序。埋在保险条款中的赔偿太薄弱，无法承担这些风险。即使是最好的赔偿条款也只与中国制造商的资产价值相当。制造商无法支付的条款毫无价值。

关于终止和存续，草案留下了太多空白，而且对不可抗力的处理是相反的。一条款项说协议除非终止否则继续有效，这本身几乎没有作用。合同应处理期限、续期、不续期通知、终止权、未补救和不可补救的违约、待处理采购订单、付款义务、模具返还、存续和终止后限制。即时终止权如果没有补救期、通知程序和“质量失败”的定义就毫无意义。存续条款也太薄弱。许多草案说保密和知识产权义务在终止后存续，但遗漏了NNN义务、模具返还、保修、赔偿、争议解决、合同损害赔偿、审计权、监管合作、召回合作和终止后使用限制。

不可抗力条款有相反的问题：它做得太多。不可抗力语言给中国供应商提供了本应属于其责任的延迟辩护。我们通常删除它，因为它往往帮助供应商而不是买方。

这个问题的最新版本是AI生成的中国合同。一家公司运行几个提示，得到一份看起来像合同的文件，然后认为已经完成了大部分工作。一些人然后用那份草案来争辩，定期起草中国制造合同的律师应该降低费用。

缺陷与美国制造协议为中国改造的缺陷相同，但通常更糟。AI草案遵循通用的法律模式。它们无法确定保修何时开始、如何校准合同损害赔偿、是否删除不可抗力，或者如何撰写在中国法院站得住脚的中文条款。它们也不会根据中国官方企业注册处核实制造商的注册中文名称、识别哪个实体持有资产，或做出决定可执行性的判断。

AI草案也往往太长，通常是中国工厂会签署的长度的两到三倍。它们不仅仅是遗漏重要条款。它们以在中国不适用的方式包含条款，这比单纯的遗漏更难发现。

危险在于草案看起来已经完成。薄的草案会显示其弱点。AI草案用流畅的措辞掩盖它们，导致买方信任一份为任何特定法律体系而建的合同。

上述问题是例子，不是完整列表。有些问题对这篇文章来说过于具体。其他问题取决于产品、供应商、付款结构或我们没有的文件。几乎我们看到的每个草案都存在同样的问题，无论是来自国内模板还是AI生成的中国合同。

你不能通过更换管辖法律条款、添加翻译然后发送给工厂来将通用美国协议改编为中国版本。你也无法通过提示进入一份有效的中国制造协议。

论坛、语言、实体验证、付款机制、模具控制、NNN条款、损害赔偿和质量程序都必须在中国法律下协同工作。这就是为什么客户草案通常增加工作量而不是减少工作量。

我们通过中国制造协议问卷更快地获得所需内容，该问卷经过数千份中国制造协议的完善，并通过一个已经用英文和中文编写并针对真实工厂抵制、模具纠纷、知识产权纠纷和付款失败的条款库测试。该系统让我们可以报出固定费用，通常大约是大型律师事务所和会计师事务所对同类中国制造协议收费的三分之一。

从国内模板开始会减慢这一过程。AI草案通常会进一步减慢。在我们使用任何内容之前，我们必须找出哪些不工作、哪些假设是错误的以及哪些保护缺失。

我们过去从客户草案工作，但我们停止了，因为在相同的固定费用下这增加了时间。现在我们只使用美国草案来提取有用的业务条款。AI草案很少给我们甚至那个。

中国制造合同是一种执行工具。它必须指定正确的中国当事方、控制语言、选择正确的论坛、设定真正的补救措施、锁定工厂对知识产权和模具的使用，并在买方的资金、模具和设计消失之前保持杠杆作用。

如果你有一份美国制造协议或AI生成的中国合同，并即将发送给中国工厂，不要因为它看起来完整就假设它保护你。为错误法律体系构建的合同可能让你比没有合同更糟糕。
articles.summary:
China Manufacturing Contracts: Why U.S. Drafts Do Not Work

Clients often send us a U.S. manufacturing agreement and ask whether it can save time or money on a China manufacturing contract. It cannot.

Sometimes the ask is smaller: can we just spend a couple of hours reviewing it? We decline because we will not bill clients for hours we know will be wasted. We may spend ten or fifteen minutes looking at what they have so we can speak to it directly. Then we tell them what we always find: using it will not decrease our time; it will increase it.

A domestic U.S. draft is built around enforcement assumptions that fail in Mainland China. The U.S. drafting lawyer may have done solid domestic work, but China manufacturing requires a contract drafted for enforcement in China.

We now get the same request about AI-generated drafts. They fail for the same reason, and for many additional reasons all their own.

We recently reviewed one such draft. It had the familiar headings: IP, tooling, warranty, compliance, dispute resolution, payment, delivery, and termination. But the provisions beneath them gave the buyer far less protection than it needed.

Familiar Terms Do Not Create China Leverage

U.S. lawyers often use familiar tools: English-language arbitration, New York or Hong Kong law, broad indemnities, insurance requirements, and U.S.-style remedies. Those provisions rarely help the buyer enforce against a Chinese factory.

A China manufacturing contract should be judged by the remedies it can support when the factory breaches. Can the buyer freeze local assets? Is the correct Chinese entity bound? Can the buyer recover its molds, tooling, or proprietary materials before they are used for someone else’s production? If the contract cannot support those remedies, it provides little leverage.

Clients often ask whether our China manufacturing contracts can be enforced in Chinese courts. Yes, they can, but if you have to sue your manufacturer, something has already gone wrong. We draft these contracts to be enforceable in China because a manufacturer that expects to lose in a Chinese court is far less likely to breach. The best China manufacturing contract is the one that prevents a lawsuit.

The contrast is straightforward:

Dispute resolution: U.S. drafts often default to Hong Kong or U.S. arbitration in English, or litigation in a local U.S. court. A China manufacturing contract usually needs a PRC court or China-based arbitration where enforcement will matter.

Governing law: U.S. drafts often use New York or Hong Kong law. For Mainland China manufacturing, PRC law usually makes more sense.

Language: U.S. drafts usually make English control. For China enforcement, the Chinese version usually should control.

Damages: U.S. drafts often rely on proving actual damages later. China manufacturing contracts should use specific contract damages tied to specific breaches.

Enforcement: U.S. drafts often assume the buyer will win first and collect later. China manufacturing contracts should preserve the ability to freeze assets and create leverage early.

IP protection: U.S. drafts usually rely on generic confidentiality and ownership language. China manufacturing contracts need NNN, non-use, anti-circumvention, and prohibitions on unauthorized China IP filings.

U.S. Law and U.S. Courts Do Not Control a Chinese Factory

The draft we reviewed called for Tennessee law, English as the controlling language, and jurisdiction in a Nashville state court. Those choices fit a domestic U.S. deal. They do little when the supplier, factory, and assets are in Mainland China.

A China manufacturing agreement should usually be built for enforcement in China: PRC law, a Chinese forum, and a Chinese-language version that controls. Without that structure, the buyer is likely to have a contract that offers little help when enforcement matters. For more on this, see How to Draft an Enforceable China Contract.

The dispute clause controls how fast the buyer can act before the molds and money are gone. Boilerplate from a domestic template is not enough.

Hong Kong and Singapore Arbitration Are Often the Wrong Default

Hong Kong and Singapore arbitration are common defaults in regional commercial agreements. For Mainland China manufacturing, they add delay and cost.

If the manufacturer and its assets are in Mainland China, a Mainland Chinese court applying PRC law to a Chinese-language contract can give the buyer direct access to pre-judgment asset preservation, bank account freezes, injunctions, and seizure of molds and equipment. The timing matters because the dispute can quickly become a collection problem.

Arbitration outside Mainland China usually adds a second enforcement step. The buyer must first obtain an arbitral award in Hong Kong or Singapore and then ask a Mainland Chinese court to recognize and enforce that award against the factory’s local assets. When the supplier, bank accounts, factory, and tooling are all in Mainland China, that extra step can cost the buyer time and leverage.

The Chinese Version Must Be Drafted for Enforcement

If litigation occurs in China, the court will work in Chinese. An English-only contract may need to be translated after the dispute begins, when technical terms, product specifications, and legal phrasing are already contested.

By then, every ambiguity costs more. If the English says one thing and the Chinese says another, the contract should state which language controls.

Translation alone is not enough. The Chinese provisions must make sense to the manufacturer and the judge. The English version still needs to reflect a China-enforcement strategy, and the Chinese version needs to match it.

The Supplier Must Be Identified and Bound Correctly

Failing to identify the supplier by its official Chinese legal name creates serious enforcement risk. The English names on invoices, websites, quotations, and email signatures are rarely the registered legal name, and using the wrong name can make the contract difficult or impossible to enforce. The contract needs to bind the exact Chinese entity that holds the business license, assets, bank account, and factory operations.

We verify the exact Chinese characters against China’s official corporate registry so the contract binds the entity we may need to sue or enforce against. The enforceable party is the Chinese legal entity, not the English name on its paperwork.

The company seal (gongzhang), commonly called the chop, is a critical execution tool. A contract signed only by an individual may invite an argument that the signer lacked authority. A contract signed by the right person and stamped with the correct chop is much harder for the supplier to deny.

The agreement should also control who performs the work and where the money goes. Buyers sometimes discover too late that the factory they thought they were using is not the factory making the product. Others discover that payment went to a Hong Kong account, a personal account, a related-company account, or another account the contracting party later claims was improper. These issues go directly to enforceability, leverage, and fraud prevention. A U.S.-style contract usually does not address these China-specific risks.

A U.S. NDA Does Not Do the Work of a China NNN Agreement

U.S. NDAs are built to stop disclosure. In China manufacturing, the greater risk is usually unauthorized use. The factory may use the buyer’s schematics, tooling, packaging, samples, or customer information to manufacture competing products through affiliates, subcontractors, or direct-to-consumer channels.

A China NNN agreement should address non-use, non-disclosure, and non-circumvention without turning those concepts into boilerplate. The NNN provisions must restrict the factory’s use of the buyer’s designs, molds, samples, CAD files, technical information, customer information, and related materials. They must also cover the factory’s affiliates, subcontractors, component suppliers, related companies, and side businesses. It often makes sense to name them.

A U.S.-style confidentiality clause may protect “confidential information,” but it rarely controls what a Chinese factory does with the buyer’s designs. See China NDA Agreements: Still Worthless After All These Years.

The agreement should also prevent the supplier, its affiliates, owners, employees, and subcontractors from filing China trademark, design patent, copyright, domain name, or similar applications based on the buyer’s brand, products, or designs. China’s first-to-file trademark system makes this especially important. See When to Register Your China Trademark: The Goldilocks Principle.

If the buyer has not registered its trademark in China, a factory or affiliate may try to register it first and use that registration as leverage. A contract should prohibit that conduct, but the better answer is usually to register the trademark in China before the factory has the opportunity. See Manufacturing in China? Register Your Trademark Before Someone Else Does.

The draft prohibited sales of “identical or similar products,” but never said what “similar” meant. A factory accused of copying or modifying a product can argue that the new version falls outside the restriction. The definition may need to cover products made with the same tooling, based on the same design files, using protected components, copying the buyer’s packaging or trade dress, or incorporating the same customer-facing features. Chinese courts will not fill that gap in the buyer’s favor, so the restriction must be in the contract.

Tooling, Molds, Samples, Prototypes, and Packaging Need Specific Protection

The draft required the Chinese manufacturer to post a deposit to secure return of the tooling, refundable only after the tooling came back in satisfactory condition. That may sound good, but nearly all Chinese factories will reject it unless the buyer has major leverage.

Tooling and mold protection works best through physical and contractual controls: clear ownership language, markings or serial numbers where appropriate, location restrictions, return deadlines, use restrictions, and defined contract damages for refusal to return or unauthorized use. The goal is terms the factory will accept and a Chinese court will enforce. See THE Guide to Molds and Tooling in International Manufacturing.

The risk starts before production begins. The factory may receive drawings, CAD files, samples, packaging designs, logos, molds, pricing data, and technical specifications before the first purchase order is issued. By then, the buyer may already have transferred its most sensitive information.

Packaging deserves its own protection. It often contains trademarks, artwork, warnings, barcodes, compliance markings, and customer-facing design. A factory that misuses packaging can create counterfeits, gray-market goods, warranty confusion, customs problems, and reputational damage. Generic IP language will not adequately control these risks.

China Contract Damages Must Be Concrete

PRC courts may adjust contract damages they view as excessive relative to actual losses. The goal is to set damages for specific breaches, such as late delivery, tooling retention, unauthorized subcontracting, or NNN violations, that are substantial enough to create leverage and credible enough for a Chinese court to enforce. See The Power of Well-Crafted China Contracts.

Getting the number right takes experience and context. Most draft contracts we see either omit contract damages or use numbers so far off that the provision may do more harm than good.

For late delivery, the contract might use a daily percentage of the order value. For failure to return tooling, it might use a fixed amount or a percentage of replacement cost. For NNN or IP violations, it might set a minimum amount per violation. The right structure depends on the product, the order value, and the likely harm.

When a Chinese manufacturer breaches, the buyer needs more than a general right to sue. It needs remedies that create leverage before the harm becomes unrecoverable.

Warranty, Inspection, and Quality Clauses Must Match the Product

The draft we reviewed used a 24-month warranty period from acceptance and noted that “the right term depends on product category and sales cycle.” That is exactly the issue: there is no standard warranty term for China manufacturing.

The right warranty depends on the product, sales channel, defect pattern, useful life, shipping timeline, and when defects appear. A warranty measured from factory acceptance may be too short for products that spend months in transit, warehousing, or seasonal retail cycles. For some products, the warranty should run from delivery, retail sale, installation, or first use.

The quality-control provisions had the same problem. Most factory disputes turn on concrete facts: defect rates, cosmetic-versus-material defects, latent failures, or shipments that barely failed inspection. A workable quality clause uses measurable standards: AQL levels, defect classifications, approved samples, testing protocols, reinspection rights, shipment holds, and a clear rule that the factory pays for failed inspections and reinspections.

Passing pre-shipment inspection should not mean final acceptance. Some defects appear only after assembly, installation, sale, or use. If the contract treats inspection as acceptance, the buyer may lose leverage before it knows whether the products work.

Control the Product, the Factory, and the Supply Chain

Chinese factories sometimes switch materials, use cheaper components, move production, or outsource work. They may be cutting costs, dealing with overload, or covering for capabilities they never had.

The contract needs a clear production baseline: approved bill of materials, reference samples, component specifications, approved suppliers, manufacturing location, and written change-control procedures. Without that baseline, the factory can argue that a cheaper substitute, different supplier, or changed production method was close enough.

Most China manufacturing contracts should also prohibit subcontracting, production moves, supplier changes, and material or component substitutions without the buyer’s written approval. Those restrictions should be backed by defined contract damages. Otherwise, when copied products appear or production shifts to an unauthorized facility, the factory can blame “another company” and leave the buyer chasing the wrong target.

Recall and Replacement-Part Issues Should Be Addressed Up Front

For consumer products, recall language matters. A general warranty clause does not cover recall cooperation, investigations, corrective action, regulatory communications, cost allocation, indemnity, or responsibility for defective or non-compliant goods.

If a product fails in the market, the buyer usually faces the customer, retailer, regulator, or plaintiff first. The contract needs to push appropriate responsibility back to the manufacturer because recall exposure can dwarf the cost of the defective goods.

Replacement parts also matter. The contract should state whether the factory must provide spare parts, for how long, at what price, in what quantities, and on what timetable. It should also cover parts needed for warranty claims, repairs, recalls, and discontinued products. Most drafts we see miss this entirely.

Purchase Orders, Payment, and Delivery Need Tight Mechanics

Many disputes arise from operational ambiguity: purchase-order acceptance, delivery dates, price changes, inspection timing, shipment approval, invoice terms, and payment conditions. A China manufacturing agreement should address these issues directly, not leave them to emails or invoices.

We typically require the manufacturer to accept or reject each purchase order in writing within a set period, with silence treated as rejection. This prevents the manufacturer from sitting on an order and later blaming the buyer for delay.

The common 30 percent deposit and 70 percent pre-shipment structure leaves the buyer with little leverage after shipment. Once the buyer has paid 100 percent before the goods leave China, it has little pressure left over warranty claims, defects, missing documents, or corrective action.

Where possible, buyers should retain part of the price until after destination inspection, delivery, or a defined post-arrival period. Even a modest holdback can change the factory’s incentives.

Payment should go only to the contracting Chinese company and only to an approved bank account. Payment to a Hong Kong account, personal account, broker account, or related-company account can create serious enforcement and fraud issues.

Late delivery needs the same discipline. A clause saying delivery dates are firm and late delivery may create damages “if applicable” states an expectation without a remedy. The contract should specify daily contract damages, cancellation rights, refunds, expedited shipping obligations, chargebacks, cover rights, and termination rights.

Compliance Language Must Be Product-Specific

The draft we reviewed included a detailed U.S. regulatory compliance section covering CPSC, ASTM, third-party test reports, Certificates of Conformance, U.S. Customs documentation, and the right to withhold final payment for missing certifications. It still needed to be tied to the actual products, standards, testing obligations, documents, failed-test consequences, and import requirements.

Compliance language should allocate testing costs, reporting deadlines, applicable standards, failed-test consequences, importability risk, inaccurate certificates, and product changes after testing.

The contract should also address import documentation, tariff classification support, country-of-origin issues, forced labor compliance, sanctions restrictions, accurate commercial invoices, packing lists, and cooperation with customs inquiries. Bad documentation can delay goods, trigger detentions, create penalties, or leave the buyer responsible for problems the factory caused.

A generic promise to “comply with all laws” is not enough when the product creates customs or forced-labor risk. The contract may need traceability records, component-origin documents, supplier certifications, and raw-material documentation. It should also allocate these risks to the factory and give the buyer remedies if the factory causes an import or customs problem.

Insurance Requirements Are No Substitute for China Leverage

The draft contract’s insurance section required commercial general liability, product liability, blank dollar limits, additional insured language, and certificates of insurance before shipment. Requiring a factory in China to add the buyer as an additional insured rarely provides practical protection.

The factory will likely price the insurance cost into the product, leaving the buyer indirectly paying for a Chinese-language policy it has not reviewed and may struggle to enforce. Our China lawyers see this often. A company tells us its Chinese factory has insurance, but when we ask whether anyone has read the Chinese policy, the answer is usually no, or yes but nobody really knows what it says.

If product liability insurance matters, the buyer is usually better off buying coverage through its own broker under a policy it can understand and control. Protection against the factory comes from contract damages, payment leverage, and the ability to pursue the factory’s assets in China. See Reducing Your Product Liability Risks from Overseas Products.

The indemnity language also missed important risks. It covered product defects, breach, and negligence or willful misconduct, but little else. For example, a factory may violate an exclusivity arrangement with another buyer and drag the new buyer into that dispute. The factory should indemnify the buyer for that.

The indemnity should also address IP claims, regulatory and customs problems, forced labor issues, recalls, subcontractor misconduct, unauthorized materials, inaccurate certifications, misuse of buyer property, and defense procedures. An indemnity buried inside an insurance paragraph is too thin to carry those risks. Even the best indemnity is only as good as the Chinese manufacturer’s assets. A clause the manufacturer cannot pay is worth little. See The Limited Value of Chinese Factory Indemnification.

Termination, Survival, and Force Majeure Can Shift Leverage the Wrong Way

On termination and survival, the draft left too many gaps, and it handled force majeure backwards. A clause saying the agreement remains in effect unless terminated does little by itself. The contract should address term, renewal, non-renewal notice, termination rights, uncured and non-curable breaches, pending purchase orders, payment obligations, tooling return, survival, and post-termination restrictions.

Immediate-termination rights mean little without a cure period, a notice procedure, and a definition of “quality failure.” The survival provisions were also too thin. Many drafts say confidentiality and IP obligations survive termination, while leaving out NNN obligations, tooling return, warranty, indemnity, dispute resolution, contract damages, audit rights, regulatory cooperation, recall cooperation, and post-termination use restrictions.

The force majeure clause had the opposite problem: it did too much. Force majeure language gives Chinese suppliers defenses for delays that should remain their responsibility. We usually leave it out because it tends to help the supplier, not the buyer. See Do Not Let Force Majeure be a Major Force In Your China Contract.

AI Drafts Have the Same Problem, Usually Worse

The newest version of this problem is the AI-generated China contract. A company runs a few prompts, gets a document that reads like a contract, and decides it has done most of the work. Some then use that draft to argue that lawyers who regularly draft China manufacturing contracts should reduce their fees.

The defect is the same as with a U.S. manufacturing agreement repurposed for China, but usually worse. AI drafts follow generic legal patterns. They cannot determine when the warranty should start, how to calibrate contract damages, whether to leave force majeure out, or how to write Chinese-language terms that hold up in a Chinese court. Nor do they verify the manufacturer’s registered Chinese name against China’s official corporate registry, identify which entity holds the assets, or make the judgment calls that determine enforceability.

AI drafts also tend to run far too long, often two or three times the length a Chinese factory will sign. They do not just leave important provisions out. They include them in ways that do not work in China, which is harder to catch than a plain omission.

The danger is that the draft looks finished. A thin draft shows its weaknesses. An AI draft hides them behind clean prose, leading the buyer to trust a contract built for no particular legal system at all.

Why Your Draft Contract Costs You More, Not Less

The issues above are examples, not a complete list. Some problems were too fact-specific for this post. Others turned on the product, supplier, payment structure, or documents we did not have.

The same problem appears in nearly every draft we see, whether it started as a domestic template or an AI-generated China contract. You cannot adapt a generic U.S. agreement for China by switching the governing law clause, adding a translation, and sending it to the factory. Nor can you prompt your way into a China manufacturing agreement that works. The forum, language, entity verification, payment mechanics, tooling controls, NNN terms, damages, and quality procedures all have to work together under Chinese law.

This is why client drafts usually increase the work rather than reduce it. We get what we need faster through our China Manufacturing Agreement questionnaire, refined over thousands of China manufacturing agreements, and through a library of provisions already written in English and Chinese and tested against real factory pushback, tooling disputes, IP fights, and payment failures.

That system lets us quote a fixed fee, often about one-third of what large law and accounting firms charge for comparable China manufacturing agreements.

Starting from a domestic template slows that process down. An AI draft usually slows it down even more. Before we can use any of it, we have to find what does not work, which assumptions are wrong, and which protections are missing.

We used to work from client drafts, but we stopped because it added time under the same flat fee. Now we use a U.S. draft only to pull out useful business terms. An AI draft rarely gives us even that.

A China manufacturing contract is an enforcement tool. It has to name the right Chinese party, control the language, choose the right forum, set real remedies, lock down the factory’s use of IP and tooling, and preserve leverage before the buyer’s money, molds, and designs are gone.

If you have a U.S. manufacturing agreement or an AI-generated China contract and are about to send it to a Chinese factory, do not assume it protects you because it looks complete. A contract built for the wrong legal system can leave you worse off than no contract at all.

The post China Manufacturing Contracts: Why Your Draft Does Not Work appeared first on Harris Sliwoski LLP.
